data.title: The h.wood Group Expands Footprint with Three New Locations Across the Los Angeles Metro Area
data.description:
The renowned hospitality group will open The Nice Guy Newport Beach, Little Luck in West Hollywood and Montana's in Brentwood

LOS ANGELES, CA / ACCESS Newswire / April 23, 2026 / The h.wood Group, the renowned Los Angeles-based hospitality company behind iconic spots like Delilah, Keys, Harriet's and more, is adding to its Southern California portfolio with the announcements of The Nice Guy Newport Beach, Montana's in Brentwood and Little Luck in West Hollywood. The Nice Guy Newport Beach, with a picturesque waterfront location, will add a second Los Angeles-area location for the Italian hot spot while Little Luck will debut as The h.wood Group's new Japanese restaurant concept and Montana's will bring a speakeasy-inspired lounge and restaurant to Brentwood.

The Nice Guy Newport Beach Rendering

The move marks a significant investment in some of the Los Angeles metropolitan area's most dynamic and high-demand neighborhoods. The expansions also reflect The h.wood Group's continued evolution from nightlife staple into a multi-market hospitality portfolio. With every location, The h.wood Group aims to create concepts that sit at the intersection of culture, entertainment, dining and design.

"Los Angeles is home for The h.wood Group and this next phase will continue our strong legacy of creating venues that blend dining, entertainment and top-tier design," said The h.wood Group Co-founder and Co-president John Terzian. "Each of these new locations represents the opportunity to create concepts that will fit effortlessly into their respective neighborhoods while remaining consistent with our approach to creating memorable experiences for guests."

Little Luck Interior Rendering

The upcoming locations span three distinct L.A. neighborhoods:

  • The Nice Guy Newport Beach marks The h.wood Group's first venture into Orange County, bringing one of its most well-known concepts into a coastal market known for its affluent consumer base and growing dining and nightlife scene. The new location will be located at 2607 West Coast Highway on Newport Beach's waterfront.

  • Little Luck will feature an elevated Japanese concept - a first for The h.wood Group. The new location at 9229 W. Sunset Boulevard will take over the space formerly held by Bootsy Bellows nightclub.

  • Montana's aims to capture the feel of an intimate clubhouse set in Brentwood. Montana's is set to open in early 2027 at 11920 San Vicente Blvd.

"We put a lot of thought into these three new locations and wanted to create them in L.A. neighborhoods where locals were looking for elevated dining concepts," said The h.wood Group Co-founder and Co-president Brian Toll. "After more than 20 years of creating hospitality venues in Los Angeles, we still get very excited to bring new offerings to guests and we think these three will be huge hits."

The announcement of these three new locations builds on The h.wood Group's continued growth after the opening of Delilah Dallas earlier this year and the recent announcement of Lady Delilah - the brand's first foray into New York City.

With development currently underway for all three Los Angeles-area projects, these latest expansions showcase The h.wood Group's commitment to its home market and continuing to serve as a leader in the luxury hospitality and dining spaces.

HI-RES RENDERINGS AVAILABLE HERE

Montana's Interior Rendering

ABOUT THE H.WOOD GROUP

The h.wood Group is a Los Angeles-based, hospitality and lifestyle marketing company with an iconic portfolio of luxury nightlife and restaurant venues. The vision of longtime friends and Los Angeles natives John Terzian and Brian Toll, The h.wood Group was founded in 2008 as a way for the consummate hosts and entrepreneurs to bring their high-end, thoughtful concepts and passion for service to life. From Delilah and The Nice Guy, to Bird Streets and Harriet's, The h.wood Group is responsible for some of the most iconic brands in the world. The h.wood Group is rapidly expanding thanks to its multi-concept portfolio uniquely positioned to cater to different markets. This growth is guided by the brand's core values - distinct design, exceptional service and palpable atmosphere - which combine to create extraordinary moments for every guest.

The h.wood Group experience is known well beyond the walls of their brick-and-mortar locations, through its work as one of the most reputable event producers in the world. With passions for art, fashion, sports and entertainment, The h.wood Group's signature approach to service and storytelling unites brands, consumers and talent at some of the world's most iconic cultural events including Coachella, F1 Las Vegas, Miami and Austin, amfAR Cannes, Art Basel and more. The h.wood Group offers corporate branding, catering, event production and marketing services at both private events. For more information, visit www.hwoodgroup.com.

Contact: hwoodpr@sequel-inc.com

SOURCE: The h.wood Group

View the original press release on ACCESS Newswire

data.title: Honeywell Cautious on Outlook Due to Ongoing War in Middle East — Market Talk
data.description: Honeywell International backs its outlook for the year, despite what CEO Vimal Kapur calls a strong start to the year. "We are taking a prudent approach to our guidance given the uncertainties surrounding the conflict in the Middle East," he says on a call with analysts. Logistical challenges and shipment delays caused a roughly 1% impact to revenue during the recent quarter, Kapur says. Despite the disruptions, the industrial conglomerate remains confident in its ability to accelerate growth during the second half of the year, as demand continues to be strong, he adds. "We continue to effectively manage through this with the safety and well-being of our employee being the top priority," Kapur says. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

data.title: London's FTSE 100 closes lower as investors monitor Iran conflict, earnings
data.description:
Britain's FTSE 100 closed a little lower on Thursday, with declines in heavyweight financials offset by some stocks after their corporate updates, while markets assessed fading prospects of renewed U.S.-Iran peace negotiations and higher oil prices.

The blue-chip FTSE 100 index CURRENCYCOM:UK100 closed 0.2% lower in its fourth session of declines, while the midcap FTSE 250 fell 0.9%.

  • Brent crude futures (LCOC1) rose beyond $100 a barrel, as Iran tightened its grip on the Strait of Hormuz and said it would not reopen the waterway until the U.S. lifted its naval blockade.

  • The travel and leisure sector (.FTNMX405010) was buoyed by a 10% gain in Domino's Pizza after it recorded a first-quarter like-for-like sales growth of 4.5%. Pressured airlines limited gains.

  • Heavyweight banks Barclays and HSBC fell 1.5% and 0.4%, respectively, the biggest drags on the index.

  • Among miners, Fresnillo declined 6.4%, tracking precious and base metals.

  • Industrial miners sector (.FTNMX551020) closed 0.9% higher on the back of a 4.1% gain in Anglo American , after a report showed its Australian coal business had at least three suitors.

  • The share of British firms reporting higher costs jumped to a record this month, signalling high input costs and rising inflation as fallout from the Iran war weighs on the economy, a survey showed.

  • Traders are now pricing in 70% probability of the Bank of England hiking rates in June, up from 40% last week, according to LSEG data.

  • The FTSE 100 is down 2% for the week so far and is on track to erase nearly all gains sparked by hopes of the U.S.–Iran ceasefire, which was announced earlier this month.

  • Among other stocks, supermarket group Sainsbury fell 3.4% after it warned that the Iran war could cloud its outlook, mirroring concerns raised by peer Tesco .

  • WH Smith plunged 9.2% to the bottom of the FTSE 250 after the travel retailer cut its annual profit forecast and suspended its dividend.

data.title: C&F Financial Q1 net income rises on community, mortgage banking segment strength
data.description:
Overview

  • U.S. regional bank's Q1 net income rose 26% yr/yr, driven by community and mortgage banking

  • Q1 EPS increased 25% yr/yr to $2.08

  • Company raised quarterly dividend 4% and repurchased $309,000 in shares

Outlook

  • Company says risks from Middle East conflict may impact interest rates and energy prices

  • C&F expects marine and recreational vehicle loan portfolio to run off over several years

  • Company says provision for credit losses may increase if loan performance deteriorates

Result Drivers

  • COMMUNITY BANKING GROWTH - Higher average loan and deposit balances, along with increased interest income, drove community banking segment results

  • MORTGAGE ORIGINATIONS SURGE - Mortgage banking segment benefited from a 58% jump in loan originations, boosting gains on sales of loans and fee income

  • CONSUMER FINANCE WEAKNESS - Segment posted a loss due to higher credit losses and increased expenses, despite higher loan yields from portfolio shift

data.title: Honeywell Nears Finish Line With Spinoff, Private-Equity Sale — Market Talk
data.description: The finish line to Honeywell's restructuring is in sight. The company is in the process of spinning off several businesses in an effort to streamline operations and carve itself into three publicly traded companies. It has now set a date of June 29 to spin off Honeywell Aerospace, after previously completing the spinoff of its advanced-materials business, Solstice Advanced Materials, in October. Honeywell additionally agrees to sell its warehouse and workflow-solutions business to private-equity firm American Industrial Partners, after earlier this week agreeing to sell its productivity solutions-and-services business to Brady. "Following these sales and spin, we will have a more cohesive portfolio focused on three principal end markets," CEO Vimal Kapur says on a call with analysts. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

data.title: Canadian dollar edges lower as Middle East war boosts producer prices
data.description:
Key points:
  • Canadian dollar falls 0.1% against the greenback
  • Trades in a range of 1.3661 to 1.3689
  • Producer prices rise 2.4% in March
  • Ten-year yield eases 3.8 basis points to 3.454%

By Fergal Smith

The Canadian dollar edged lower for a third straight day against its U.S. counterpart on Thursday, as investors weighed continued restrictions on trade through the Strait of Hormuz and data that showed resulting higher oil prices boosted domestic producer prices last month.

The loonie was trading 0.1% lower at 1.3680 per U.S. dollar, or 73.10 U.S. cents, after moving in a range of 1.3661 to 1.3689.

"Domestic news and developments remain limited and the intraday trend in the CAD continues to depend primarily on the broader dollar tone and the risk backdrop," Shaun Osborne and Eric Theoret, strategists at Scotiabank, said in a note.

"We continue to look for limited USD gains and firm resistance in the low/mid-1.37 zone. Support remains (at) 1.3625 ahead of a drop back to the low 1.35s."

The U.S. dollar edged higher against a basket of major currencies as a stand-off between Iran and the U.S. in the Middle East war and a lack of progress on peace talks sent oil prices higher and dented investor optimism.

The price of oil , one of Canada's major exports, was trading 1.5% higher at $94.37 a barrel.

Canadian producer prices rose 2.4% in March from February on higher prices for energy and petroleum products, as well as chemical products, linked to the closure of the strait.

Higher sales in the petroleum and coal product sub-sector and the transportation equipment sub-sector helped lift factory sales by 3.5% in March from February, a preliminary estimate showed.

Retail sales data for February, due on Friday, could offer additional clues on the state of the domestic economy, ahead of a Bank of Canada interest rate decision next Wednesday.

Investors are betting that the central bank will leave its benchmark interest rate on hold at 2.25% but then hike once before the end of the year.

Canadian government bond yields moved lower across a flatter curve, with the 10-year (CA10YT=RR) down 3.8 basis points at 3.454%.

data.title: France's Savencia Q1 sales dip on comparable basis
data.description:
Overview

  • France dairy and cheese producer's Q1 sales declined 0.4% on a comparable basis

  • Cheese product sales fell 1.4% on a comparable basis, other dairy products rose 0.3%

  • Sales decline mainly due to unfavorable exchange rates and lower butter, industrial prices

Outlook

  • Savencia says 2026 outlook remains uncertain and volatile due to geopolitical and economic risks

  • Company expects rising energy costs and inflation may impact consumption in Europe

  • Savencia will continue specialty strategy and invest in business complementarity and brand growth

Result Drivers

  • EXCHANGE RATES - Co said sales were significantly impacted by unfavorable exchange rates, mainly due to the devaluation of the Argentine peso and Asian currencies

  • CHEESE PRICING PRESSURE - Cheese product sales were affected by negative price effects and milk price variations, despite resilient volumes and international market performance

  • INDUSTRIAL PRICE DECLINE - Other dairy products were penalised by a sharp decline in industrial product quotations and lower butter prices, though ingredient volumes rose

Company press release:

data.title: Siemens Energy raises outlook as demand for power equipment soars
data.description:
Siemens Energy on Thursday raised its outlook for 2026, joining peers in benefiting from a surge in demand for data centres that require power equipment the German company supplies.

Citing "positive business development" in the first six months of its financial year from October 1, and strong market demand, Siemens Energy said it now expects sales to grow by 14-16%, compared with 11-13% previously.

The company's profit margin before special items is now forecast at 10-12%, up from 9-11%.

The boost in demand for gas turbines and grid components, fuelled by energy-hungry data centres, also saw U.S. rival GE Vernova raise its annual revenue and profit forecasts on Wednesday.

Siemens Energy shares hit a fresh record high earlier on Thursday, making it Germany's third most valuable company after former parent Siemens and SAP with a market value of around 158 billion euros.

data.title: Siemens Energy Upgrades 2026 Outlook After Rise in Quarterly Net Profit, Revenue
data.description:
(15:56 GMT) *Siemens Energy Posts Prelim Figures
(15:56 GMT) *Siemens Energy Raises FY26 View
(15:57 GMT) *Siemens Energy Sees FY26 Comparable Rev Growth Between 14% and 16%, Prior 11% to 13%
(15:57 GMT) *Siemens Energy: This Excludes Currency Translation and Portfolio Effects
(15:58 GMT) *Siemens Energy Sees FY26 Profit Margin Before Special Items Between 10% and 12%, Prior 9% to 11%

(16:00 GMT) *Siemens Energy Sees FY26 Pretax FCF of Around EUR8B, Prior EUR4B to EUR5B
(16:01 GMT) *Siemens Energy 2Q Orders EUR17.75B
(16:02 GMT) *Siemens Energy 2Q Rev EUR10.29B
(16:03 GMT) *Siemens Energy 2Q Net Pft EUR1.11B
(16:04 GMT) *Siemens Energy 2Q Net Income EUR835M
(16:04 GMT) *Siemens Energy to Publish Full Earnings on May 12
(16:16 GMT) Siemens Energy Upgrades 2026 Outlook After Rise in Quarterly Net Profit, Revenue

By Cristina Gallardo

Siemens Energy raised its outlook for fiscal 2026 after posting an increase in second-quarter net profit and revenue.

The German maker of energy equipment said Thursday that it now expects comparable revenue growth of between 14% and 16% for the year ending Sept. 30, up from a previous estimate of between 11% and 13%.

The company's profit margin before special items should range between 10% and 12% for the fiscal year, versus a previous estimate of between 9% and 11%, it said.

Net income for the year should be around 4 billion euros ($4.68 billion), up from a previous expectation of between 3 billion and 4 billion euros, Siemens Energy said.

The upgraded guidance comes after the German group posted preliminary figures for its second quarter, in which net profit came at 1.11 billion euros, up from 615 million euros in the same period of the prior year.

Second-quarter revenue rose to 10.29 billion euros from 9.96 billion euros a year earlier, but was below a consensus estimate of 10.8 billion euros provided by FactSet.

Write to Cristina Gallardo at cristina.gallardo@wsj.com

data.title: U.S. Airlines Raise Fares, Trim Capacity for Summer as Fuel Costs Jump
data.description:
By Giulia Petroni

U.S. airlines are raising fares and baggage fees to offset higher fuel costs from the Iran war. Some are also cutting capacity heading into the busy summer travel season.

Average jet fuel prices for delivery into major U.S. hubs have climbed since the start of the war--rising to $4.23 a gallon currently from around $2.50 before the conflict, according to Argus price data--reflecting how heavily global supply depends on refining capacity in the Persian Gulf.

As a result, airline fuel costs are rising sharply. American Airlines said its fuel bill could jump by $4 billion this year, putting pressure on profitability, while Delta expects its fuel spending to increase by $2 billion this quarter.

All major U.S. carriers have increased checked-baggage fees in recent weeks--with charges ranging from $4 to $10 more for first bags--but not all have announced schedule changes. United Airlines is cutting around 5% of planned flights, mainly targeting less-profitable redeye flights and midweek travel, and said it might need to raise ticket prices by 15% to 20%. Delta said it would "meaningfully" cut capacity growth to make up for higher fuel costs.

Jet fuel typically accounts for a quarter or more of airlines' operating expenses, according to the International Air Transport Association, leaving carriers vulnerable when prices rise faster than pre-sold ticket fares.

The fuel typically moves globally via ocean tankers, pipelines, rail and trucks from refineries to storage terminals and airports. Pricing is anchored to crude oil benchmarks, then adjusted for refining costs, regional supply and demand, and transport logistics.

As supply constraints in the Middle East ripple across regions, pricing dynamics are dictating where the pinch hits hardest.

One area of concern is the U.S. West Coast, which is more heavily reliant on imports--mainly from South Korea--to meet jet fuel demand. The region currently imports around 15% to 20% of its jet fuel compared with 0% to 5% in other U.S. regions, according to the American Petroleum Institute.

With Asia more exposed to crude shortages due to disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz, Chinese exports have been constrained and South Korean refineries are running at a lower operating rate, said George Shaw, senior middle distillates analyst at data provider Kpler. As a result, West Coast inventories could begin to decline in the coming weeks, though they currently remain above year-ago levels.

The U.S., a net exporter of jet fuel, has stepped up production and overseas shipments since the war in Iran began. Data from the U.S. Energy Information Administration showed exports reached 442,000 barrels a day in the week ended on April 3, up from around 278,000 barrels a day a year earlier.

Those shipments could help offset shortages in Europe, where disruptions through the Strait of Hormuz have cut off a significant share of supply and forced airlines to trim flight schedules.

Last week, the International Energy Agency warned that Europe may have only about six weeks of jet fuel left before shortages. Germany's Deutsche Lufthansa already said it would cut 20,000 short-haul flights from its schedule through October, while Dutch airline KLM canceled 160 flights to and from Amsterdam for May.

The Middle East has typically supplied up to 75% of Europe's net jet fuel imports. However, even a rapid acceleration in U.S. jet fuel exports would only partially fill the gap, replacing just over 50% of lost Middle East volumes, according to the IEA.

Constraints on infrastructure could also represent a challenge. Differences in fuel specifications, limited tank storage and pipeline capacity mean tanks would likely need to be converted and cleaned before handling additional volumes, said Kpler's Shaw.

Even if disruptions ease, analysts say it could take months for supplies to normalize.

"The tightness in the jet fuel market is not likely to abate any time soon," Shaw said. "Even if the Strait reopens promptly, significant damage to infrastructure, vessel avoidance of the Gulf and time for supply to reach markets will keep jet fuel prices elevated globally and these costs will be passed on to the consumer."

Write to Giulia Petroni at giulia.petroni@wsj.com
